claims.kill_condition: A prior source already defines and empirically validates the same four-part fractional attribution under materially equivalent estimands.
claims.name: four_mechanism_fractional_attribution
claims.proposed_claim: Observed skin-imaging information loss can be quantitatively partitioned into measurement noise, spectral projection, biological collinearity and model mismatch using one integrated adjudication framework.

claims.kill_condition: Prior work performs materially equivalent real-skin-spectrum camera-operator observability and metamer enumeration.
claims.name: real_spectra_camera_observability
claims.proposed_claim: Empirical human skin reflectance spectra can be projected through camera-illuminant operators to quantify observable, weakly observable and metameric biological variation.

claims.kill_condition: Prior work estimates the same recoverable-versus-unrecoverable boundary across calibrated spectra and uncontrolled images.
claims.name: capture_control_boundary
claims.proposed_claim: A measurable boundary can be estimated between variation recoverable through capture control and variation that remains unrecoverable because of observation-operator information loss.

claims.kill_condition: Prior work performs the same three-component quantitative decomposition with external pathology-confirmed validation.
claims.name: physics_contrast_data_quality_decomposition
claims.proposed_claim: Clinical performance disparities can be decomposed into measurement-physics, visible-contrast and dataset-quality components under patient-disjoint external validation.

claims.kill_condition: Prior work already establishes the same subject-balanced, body-site-conditioned representation and its downstream measurement consequences.
claims.name: conditioned_skin_spectral_representation
claims.proposed_claim: Skin spectral structure should be estimated conditionally on body site and subject identity rather than represented by a single global skin-tone scalar.

claims.kill_condition: Prior work implements a materially equivalent governed cross-domain evidence chain with explicit asset-role limits.
claims.name: cross_domain_evidence_chain
claims.proposed_claim: A governed evidence chain can connect calibrated spectral metrology, camera projection, repeated capture, heterogeneous clinical images and pathology-confirmed external validation without treating those assets as interchangeable ground truth.

Detect lesion identity appearing in multiple partitions.
Move overlapping lesion images to a single partition to create DermaMNIST-C.

Compute image embeddings and pairwise cosine similarities using fastdup.
Manually inspect high-similarity pairs and clusters.
Remove duplicates and erroneous images and standardize partitions to create Fitzpatrick17k-C.
Repeat classification benchmarks on original and corrected datasets.

evidence_spans.support: The abstract states the audit, correction, and reproducibility contributions.
The concluding discussion restates the corrected and extended datasets.

Release of DermaMNIST-C, DermaMNIST-E, and Fitzpatrick17k-C
Public release of analysis code and corrected datasets

field_observations.review_notes: No optical, camera, colorimetric, or spectral calibration procedure is defined.
No camera spectral-sensitivity, radiometric-response, exposure, or device-calibration model is provided.
The source audits previously acquired images and does not prescribe a new capture protocol.
Melanin, hemoglobin, and other chromophores are not modeled or estimated.

evidence_spans.support: Lesion identifiers are used to detect and correct cross-partition leakage.
fastdup embedding similarity and manual review are used to classify duplicate and nonduplicate pairs.
The Fitzpatrick17k analysis uses similarity thresholds and clustering to identify duplicate pairs and groups.
ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 benchmarks are repeated on original, corrected, and extended datasets.

audit_components: Metadata-based lesion and image identity analysis
Cosine-similarity ranking followed by manual visual confirmation
Duplicate clustering using a similarity threshold

evidence_spans.support: The three audited datasets are named and their analysis scope is stated.
HAM10000 is described as containing 10,015 dermoscopic images.
The source summarizes original and corrected dataset versions, including Fitzpatrick17k's 16,577 images.

audited.name: HAM10000
audited.role: Source dataset for DermaMNIST and lesion-identity analysis

The Fitzpatrick17k analysis applies similarity thresholds and cluster criteria.

value: Duplicate-pair or duplicate-cluster threshold based on similarity at or above approximately 0.90

evidence_spans.support: Partition leakage is quantified by images and lesions.
The benchmark reports AUC and overall accuracy.
Duplicate analysis reports similarity, pairs, and clusters.

field_observations.review_notes: Illumination variation appears as a possible source of visual duplication or segmentation difficulty, but no illuminant model or controlled-light assumption is defined.
The study analyzes existing image datasets and does not specify a canonical acquisition instrument or camera system.

evidence_spans.support: DermaMNIST is traced to HAM10000 and its image-resolution transformation.
Image-pair similarity is compared with lesion-identity metadata.
Fitzpatrick17k image and label content is examined for duplication and mislabeling.

value: Dermoscopic images and metadata from HAM10000 and DermaMNIST
Clinical dermatology images and labels from Fitzpatrick17k

evidence_spans.support: The study reports image and lesion overlap across dataset partitions.
Cosine similarity and manually confirmed duplicate classes are observed.
AUC and accuracy are the reported benchmark metrics.
The source reports diagnosis-label, skin-type-label, duplicate, and split corrections.

field_observations.review_notes: The study is a dataset-quality and benchmark audit. The source does not define a light-tissue physical model.

evidence_spans.support: The abstract identifies duplicates, partition leakage, mislabeling, and test-partition problems as the study target.
The study scope explicitly covers DermaMNIST, HAM10000, and Fitzpatrick17k and reports correction of identified defects.

field_observations.value: Quality defects in widely used dermatological image datasets, including duplicate images, train-validation-test leakage, mislabeled images, metadata errors, inappropriate image resizing, and absent or unsuitable held-out test partitions.

evidence_spans.support: The source documents threshold-bound duplicate detection and manual-review limitations.
The discussion identifies benchmark noncomparability, threshold misses, manual-review limits, and segmentation challenges.

value: Similarity thresholds may miss true duplicates whose scores fall below the selected cutoff.
The scale of manual review prevents a guarantee that Fitzpatrick17k-C is entirely free of duplicates.
Automated healthy-skin segmentation is sensitive to illumination, image quality, multiple objects, and diffuse pathology boundaries.
DermaMNIST and DermaMNIST-C benchmark values should not be directly compared because their training and evaluation partitions differ.

evidence_spans.support: The source reports exact image and lesion overlap counts across DermaMNIST partitions.
The source reports AUC and accuracy for original, corrected, and extended DermaMNIST versions.
The source summarizes corrected dataset sizes, split composition, and the fraction of DermaMNIST lesions affected by leakage.

value.benchmark_observation: Corrected and extended datasets produce materially different AUC and accuracy profiles; DermaMNIST-E is more challenging than the original benchmark.

evidence_spans.support: HAM10000 includes multiple images of lesions acquired at two clinical sites.
Leakage is quantified using images and unique lesion identifiers.
Fitzpatrick17k duplicate analysis is performed at image-pair and cluster levels.

field_observations.value: The unit of analysis is the image, lesion identity, image pair, or duplicate cluster. The article does not establish a unified subject-level cohort across the three datasets.

field_observations.review_notes: Optical scattering is not modeled or estimated.

evidence_spans.support: The authors state that they uncover quality issues, measure benchmark effects, propose corrections, and release the analysis pipeline.
The closing interpretation restates the corrected and extended datasets and the objective of exposing hidden data-quality defects.

field_observations.value: Systematically audit DermaMNIST, its source dataset HAM10000, and Fitzpatrick17k; quantify the effects of identified quality defects; and release corrected datasets and reproducible analysis code.

evidence_spans.support: DermaMNIST and HAM10000 are image and lesion-label datasets.
Fitzpatrick17k includes clinical images, diagnosis labels, and Fitzpatrick skin-type labels.

field_observations.value: Skin is represented through existing dermoscopic or clinical RGB images, disease labels, lesion identities, and Fitzpatrick skin-type labels. No continuous spectral skin representation is introduced.

evidence_spans.support: The paper explicitly links dataset quality defects with benchmark reliability.
Partition leakage is shown to threaten benchmark validity.
The discussion details data, split, label, and evaluation limitations.
Public data and code support a traceable evidence chain.

value.not_established: The paper does not establish a physics-based skin-color decomposition, spectral reflectance recovery method, camera observability model, capture control boundary, or conditioned skin spectral representation.
value.relevance: The study establishes that duplicate identity, partition leakage, label integrity, image-processing provenance, and explicit train-validation-test construction materially affect reported dermatology-model performance. It supports TrueColor's requirement to separate data-quality and evidence-chain failures from model or physical-model performance.

evidence_spans.support: Candidate duplicates are verified through manual review.
Benchmark experiments are repeated three times and reported as mean plus or minus standard deviation.

value.benchmark_validation: ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 classification experiments repeated three times
value.duplicate_validation: Embedding-similarity detection followed by manual visual confirmation
value.reported_summary: Mean and standard deviation across repeated benchmark runs

melanin.parameterization: Volume fraction, extinction coefficient, and concentration

Absorption coefficient parameterized by chromophore extinction and concentration
Reduced scattering coefficient containing Mie and Rayleigh terms

evidence_spans.support: The source defines epidermal melanin absorption, dermal hemoglobin absorption, and reduced scattering.

layers.dermis: Hemoglobin absorption layer with multiple scattering

value.relationship: Skin reflectance is generated from epidermal transmission and dermal reflection, then combined with illuminant SPD and camera spectral sensitivity to produce RGB values.

evidence_spans.support: The abstract identifies the limitations of clinical equipment and states the proposed decomposition and reconstruction approach.
The introduction describes dedicated acquisition systems and motivates a more extensible model.

field_observations.value: Existing clinical skin-analysis systems can estimate melanin and hemoglobin reliably but are acquisition-system-specific, costly, and computationally intensive. The paper addresses image-based recovery of skin-property maps while preserving the input image resolution.

evidence_spans.support: The discussion explicitly identifies comparison, detail, morphology, prior-knowledge, dataset, and SPD limitations.

value: The model requires prior knowledge of the light-source SPD and camera information.
VISIA and the proposed method produce maps in different output formats, requiring conversion before comparison.
Postprocessing and normalization choices can influence correlation.
The proposed maps capture less fine detail than VISIA.
The specular map does not yet preserve all morphological detail needed for wrinkle or pore diagnosis.
Broader datasets and more accurate spectrometer-based SPD measurements are needed to improve accuracy.

evidence_spans.support: The source reports reconstruction PSNR and map behavior over training.
The source reports positive correlations with VISIA and identifies VISIA's superior fine-detail capture.

field_observations.review_notes: Exact per-map correlation values are not asserted here because the selected packet output does not expose them unambiguously as plain text.

value.clinical_comparison: Predicted melanin and hemoglobin maps show strong positive relationships with VISIA maps.
value.map_detail: VISIA captures finer detailed features than the proposed model.
value.reconstruction: Average PSNR exceeds 30, which the authors interpret as high similarity between input and reconstructed images.
value.training_interpretation: Because VISIA maps were not used during training, the authors interpret the correlation as evidence that the network learned aspects of the intended light–tissue interaction.

evidence_spans.support: The subject counts and train-test division are stated.

value.population_demographics: Not sufficiently established in the selected source ranges
value.unit_of_analysis: Subject facial images and spatial skin maps

field_observations.value: Train a deep network to solve a skin light–tissue forward problem by decomposing facial RGB images into melanin, hemoglobin, shading, and specular maps and reconstructing the input image from those components.

evidence_spans.support: The source explicitly defines four spatial output maps.

value.spatial_form: Pixel-aligned two-dimensional maps at the input image resolution

evidence_spans.support: The physical, chromophore, scattering, camera, illuminant, and image-formation mechanisms are explicit.
The paper explicitly retains camera and illuminant information as required priors.

value.boundary: The method operates with known illuminant SPD and known camera spectral sensitivity. It does not establish blind recovery under unknown lighting and unknown device response, nor a device-independent mobile observability certificate.

evidence_spans.support: The abstract and introduction define RGB-to-HSI spectral reconstruction and state that the inverse process is severely ill-posed.

field_observations.value: Recovering a high-dimensional hyperspectral image from a three-channel RGB image. The inverse mapping is severely ill-posed because multiple spectra can produce the same RGB observation.

Known and unknown camera-response conditions are distinguished.

value: The RGB-to-HSI inverse problem is severely ill-posed.
The CSS prior requires a known camera spectral-sensitivity function.
The CSS prior cannot be applied to the Real World benchmark because its camera response is unknown.
The method is trained and evaluated on benchmark camera-response conditions and does not infer an unseen device response.
The paper does not address illumination variation as a separate latent variable.

field_observations.value: The handheld colorimeter is treated as insensitive to ambient lighting because its measurement system supplies and controls its own measurement illumination. The investigators also tested palette measurements across locations and found no location effect.

evidence_spans.support: The introduction describes ambiguity, misclassification, inconsistent scale use, and underrepresentation of Hispanic and Black populations.

field_observations.value: Conventional self-reported skin-color and sun-sensitivity scales were developed largely for White populations, may be interpreted inconsistently, and can misclassify skin color across racial and ethnic groups.

field_observations.review_notes: The final limitation concerning representation granularity is an evidence-grounded methodological boundary: the paper explicitly defines a nine-point scale compared with a continuous instrument index.
value: The population was predominantly White and/or Hispanic.
Few participants had melanin-index values above 70.

Explicit distinction between exposed and unexposed anatomical sites
Population inclusion and darker-skin coverage limitations

evidence_spans.support: The abstract identifies image-only classification limits, skin-tone bias, smartphone variability, dataset imbalance, and the benchmark-to-clinic gap.
The review describes degradation across devices, image quality, clinical settings, populations, lighting, distance, compression, and background pigmentation.

field_observations.value: Single-image dermatology classifiers omit morphology, distribution, symptoms, temporal evolution, history, pathology, treatment response, and acquisition context. Their performance may also degrade across skin tones, devices, lighting conditions, image quality, disease spectra, and care settings.

evidence_spans.support: The abstract identifies core scientific and deployment limitations.
The review details capture variability, shortcuts, weak labels, bias, and real-world deployment limitations.
The conclusion reiterates acquisition, skin-tone, context, and prospective-validation barriers.

value: Single-image systems omit clinically necessary context for many dermatologic differentials.
Curated benchmark performance may fail under real-world devices, lighting, compression, distance, and image-quality variation.
Darker skin, rare disorders, acral disease, mucosal disease, and limited-resource settings are underrepresented.
Fitzpatrick labels incompletely represent skin color and optical visibility.
Datasets may encode device, ruler, marker, background, referral, workup, and spectrum shortcuts.
Reference standards may be mixed, noisy, or dependent on follow-up and treatment response.
Aggregate AUC does not establish fairness, clinical utility, safety, or deployment readiness.
The proposed multimodal architecture is not implemented or validated in this paper.
The paper reports no original cohort, dataset, experiment, or quantitative performance.

evidence_spans.support: The review synthesizes dataset-to-clinic, skin-tone visibility, and fairness findings.

synthesized_findings: Image-only models lose reliability across devices, image quality, settings, and patient populations
Dermatology AI can perform worse on datasets enriched for darker skin tones
Post-hoc reweighting may not fully correct models trained on narrow data
Fitzpatrick labels do not capture the full range of color, hue, undertone, and imaging contrast
Automatic white balance and smartphone color processing can alter erythema and pigmentation
Curated benchmark AUC does not establish clinical readiness

directly_relevant_findings: Skin pigmentation changes the visibility of erythema, vascular structures, pigment networks, and inflammatory findings

Lighting, distance, compression, cosmetics, and device variation alter the observable signal
Fitzpatrick labels do not represent the full range of skin color, hue, undertone, and imaging contrast

exclusion_criteria: source has no material relationship to a registered claim
source is purely promotional without technical disclosure
source duplicates another version without additional content
inclusion_criteria: source addresses at least one registered TrueColor claim
method or result is described with enough detail for overlap adjudication
source identity and publication status can be verified
primary sources are preferred over summaries
document.name: prior_art_extraction_and_novelty_boundary
document.objective: Determine whether each proposed TrueColor contribution is already disclosed, substantially overlapping, incrementally different, genuinely novel, or not yet adjudicable.
